definition: JSON Web Token — a compact, URL-safe token format (header.payload.signature) used to transmit claims between parties, commonly for API authentication and single sign-on.
JSON Web Signature — the signed JWT format defined in RFC 7515. Most JWTs in production are JWS tokens with a cryptographic signature.
JSON Web Encryption — an encrypted token format (RFC 7516) where the payload is encrypted, not just signed. Used when token contents must remain confidential.
JSON Web Key — a JSON data structure representing a cryptographic key. Used in JWKS documents for RS256/ES256 verification.
JSON Web Key Set — a JSON document containing an array of public keys (JWKs). OIDC providers publish JWKS at /.well-known/jwks.json for token verification.
A name/value pair inside the JWT payload. Registered claims (exp, sub, iss) are standardized; private claims are custom to your application.
The first segment of a JWT, Base64URL-encoded JSON typically containing alg (algorithm) and typ (token type). Always verify alg before trusting a token.
The second segment of a JWT containing claims — user ID, roles, expiration, issuer, and custom data. Readable by anyone; never store secrets here.
The third segment of a JWT, created by signing header.payload with a secret (HS256) or private key (RS256). Proves integrity and authenticity.
Algorithm header claim specifying how the signature was created (e.g. HS256, RS256, ES256). Must match your verification method.
Expiration Time claim — Unix timestamp after which the token must be rejected. Always validate server-side.
Issuer claim — identifies who created the token, usually your auth server URL. Validate against expected issuer.
Audience claim — identifies intended recipients (API identifier or client ID). Prevents token misuse across services.
Subject claim — unique identifier for the user or entity the token represents.
Issued At claim — Unix timestamp when the token was created. Useful for freshness checks and debugging.
Not Before claim — Unix timestamp before which the token must not be accepted. Prevents premature use.
JWT ID claim — unique token identifier for revocation lists and replay prevention.
Key ID header parameter — references which key in a JWKS was used to sign the token.
HMAC-SHA256 symmetric signing algorithm. Same secret signs and verifies. Simple but requires secure secret distribution.
RSA-SHA256 asymmetric signing. Private key signs; public key verifies. Standard for OIDC and enterprise auth.
ECDSA with P-256 and SHA-256. Compact keys, widely used by Apple, Google, and modern OIDC providers.
URL-safe Base64 encoding used in JWTs. Replaces +/ with -_ and omits padding. Not encryption — anyone can decode.
HTTP Authorization scheme where the client sends Authorization: Bearer <token>. Whoever holds the token gains access.
Short-lived token authorizing API access. OAuth 2.0 access tokens are often JWTs but can be opaque strings.
Long-lived token used to obtain new access tokens without re-authentication. Must be stored securely and rotatable.
OpenID Connect token containing user identity claims. Always a JWT. Not used for API authorization — use access tokens instead.
Identity layer on OAuth 2.0. Adds ID tokens, standardized claims, and discovery endpoints including JWKS.
Authorization framework for delegated access. Often issues JWT access tokens but does not require JWT format.
OAuth permission string (e.g. openid profile email) defining what access a token grants.
OAuth endpoint (/oauth/token) where clients exchange credentials or codes for access and refresh tokens.
Small time difference between servers. JWT libraries typically allow 30–60 seconds leeway on exp/nbf validation.
Security vulnerability where attackers set alg:none to bypass signature verification. Always reject unsigned tokens.
Periodic replacement of signing keys. JWKS may contain multiple keys; kid header identifies the active one.
Invalidating tokens before expiry via blocklists, short TTLs, or refresh token rotation. JWTs are stateless — revocation requires extra design.
Proof Key for Code Exchange — OAuth extension preventing authorization code interception. Required for public clients (SPAs, mobile).
Standard path for OIDC discovery (/.well-known/openid-configuration) and JWKS (/.well-known/jwks.json) endpoints.
The IETF standard defining JSON Web Token (JWT) structure and registered claims.
The IETF standard for JSON Web Signature (JWS) — how JWT signatures are created and verified.
